nl_question: What phenotypic features does cystic fibrosis cause?
predicate_note: Edge direction: Disease (subject) → causes → PhenotypicFeature (object). Original natural-language `biolink:has_phenotype` returns 0 results for MONDO:0009061 in KG2.10.2c despite being in the meta-knowledge-graph; gold uses `biolink:causes` for the same semantic relation, per the q9 predicate_fix in the legacy gold record. Note also that PloverDB returns a mix of HP, MONDO, NCIT, UMLS, and EFO CURIEs in the PhenotypicFeature slot — KG2c cross-categorizes some disease/symptom terms as phenotypic features.

validation.predicate_fix: has_phenotype -> causes (has_phenotype returns 0 for this entity despite being in meta KG)

verification_guide.summary: 4 cystic-fibrosis phenotypes spanning 4 organ systems (pulmonary / pulmonary structural / GI-pancreatic / genitourinary), each named explicitly in the GeneReviews chapter for Cystic Fibrosis (NBK1250). Table 2 of NBK1250 provides authoritative frequency statistics. Each is in PloverDB's 25-result PhenotypicFeature set for cystic fibrosis.
sources_used: Cutting GR. Cystic Fibrosis. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., eds. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 2001 Mar 26 [last revised 2024 Aug 8]. URL: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1250/
verification_guide.how_to_verify_an_entry: Open the GeneReviews URL. The chapter is organized as: Summary → Diagnosis → Clinical Characteristics (with Table 2 "Frequency of Select Features") → Clinical Description (with Respiratory / Otolaryngology / Exocrine Pancreas / etc. subsections) → Management → and so on. For each phenotype, the `where_on_page` field points to either the Summary's single multisystem-disease sentence (which lists nearly all CF morbidities together), Table 2 (which gives % frequencies), or the relevant Clinical Description subsection. The exact_quote field reproduces one or two sentences verbatim from that location.
verification_guide.cross_check_with_ploverdb: Each verified_answer's CURIE (HP:0006528, MONDO:0001684, MONDO:0004822, MONDO:0005372) was confirmed present in the PloverDB result set for the gold one-hop query (n0=MONDO:0009061 cystic fibrosis, n1=biolink:PhenotypicFeature, e0=biolink:causes from disease to phenotype). PloverDB returned 25 PhenotypicFeature CURIEs total — 3 HP terms (Dehydration, Chronic lung disease, Chronic infection), 14 MONDO terms (most of the verified set), 1 EFO term (complication), 2 NCIT terms (Inflammation, Obstruction), and 5 UMLS terms (Diabetes, Chronic inflammation, Meconium plug syndrome, Functional disorder, Terminal illness).
verification_guide.intentionally_omitted: PloverDB also returns several less-specific or coarse-grained terms (NCIT:C3137 Inflammation, NCIT:C3284 Obstruction, EFO:0009518 complication, UMLS:C0277785 Functional disorder, UMLS:C0679247 Terminal illness, UMLS:C0021376 Chronic inflammation). These are biologically true of CF but too generic to function as informative gold answers — they would match almost any chronic systemic disease. They are documented but not included in the verified_answers set. UMLS:C0270246 (Meconium plug syndrome) was considered, but NBK1250 mentions only "meconium ileus" — these are clinically related but UMLS treats them as distinct concepts, so the GeneReviews text does not strictly support the C0270246 label. UMLS:C0011847 (Diabetes) is well-supported by Table 2 (18% frequency) and would be a valid 5th answer — added below as an extended-set option for breadth, but the 4-phenotype core set already spans 4 organ systems.

evidence.publisher: University of Washington, Seattle — GeneReviews® (NCBI Bookshelf)
evidence.section_heading: Clinical Description → Respiratory → Pulmonary (Table 2 also lists 100% frequency)
evidence.citation: Cutting GR. Cystic Fibrosis. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 2001 Mar 26 [last revised 2024 Aug 8]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1250/
evidence.where_on_page: Open the URL. Scroll past the Summary to the "Clinical Characteristics → Clinical Description" section. "Table 2. Cystic Fibrosis: Frequency of Select Features" sits at the top of that section; its first data row reads "Lung disease 100%". Immediately below the table, the "Respiratory" subsection opens with a paragraph headed "Pulmonary." — its first sentence is the cited quote.
evidence.exact_quote: Lung disease is the major cause of morbidity and mortality in people with CF.

verified_answers.label: bronchiectasis
verified_answers.frequency_in_cf: essentially universal in established CF lung disease (NBK1250 Pulmonary section; may be detected in infants as young as 10 weeks)

evidence.publisher: University of Washington, Seattle — GeneReviews® (NCBI Bookshelf)
evidence.citation: Cutting GR. Cystic Fibrosis. In: Adam MP, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 2001 Mar 26 [last revised 2024 Aug 8]. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1250/
evidence.where_on_page: Open the URL and scroll to the "Pulmonary." paragraph inside the Clinical Description → Respiratory section (immediately after Table 2). Read down two sentences — the third sentence of the paragraph describes how thickened airway surface layer attracts bacteria, and the next sentence is the cited quote. The Summary paragraph at the top of the chapter also names bronchiectasis ("progressive obstructive pulmonary disease with bronchiectasis"), but the Respiratory subsection contains the mechanistic-description sentence used as the exact_quote.
evidence.exact_quote: The resulting thickened airway surface layer attracts bacteria; subsequent white blood cell reaction leads to bronchiectasis (an abnormal dilatation of the airways). Bronchiectasis may be detected in infants as early as age ten weeks.

evidence.publisher: University of Washington, Seattle — GeneReviews® (NCBI Bookshelf)
evidence.section_heading: Clinical Characteristics → Clinical Description → Table 2 (Frequency of Select Features) + Summary paragraph
evidence.citation: Cutting GR. Cystic Fibrosis. In: Adam MP, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 2001 Mar 26 [last revised 2024 Aug 8]. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1250/
evidence.where_on_page: Open the URL. Two corroborating locations: (a) "Table 2. Cystic Fibrosis: Frequency of Select Features" near the top of Clinical Characteristics — its third data row reads "Pancreatic insufficiency ~85%"; (b) the Summary paragraph at the very top of the chapter lists "exocrine pancreatic deficiency and malnutrition" as one of the listed morbidities. The exact_quote below combines a phrase from the Summary with the Table 2 frequency.
evidence.exact_quote: Morbidities include ... exocrine pancreatic deficiency and malnutrition, pancreatitis ... [Table 2:] Pancreatic insufficiency ~85%.

verified_answers.label: male infertility
verified_answers.frequency_in_cf: ~95-98% of males with classic CF (congenital bilateral absence of vas deferens, CBAVD); NBK1250 Summary names the mechanism but does not give a numeric frequency

evidence.publisher: University of Washington, Seattle — GeneReviews® (NCBI Bookshelf)
evidence.citation: Cutting GR. Cystic Fibrosis. In: Adam MP, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 2001 Mar 26 [last revised 2024 Aug 8]. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1250/
evidence.where_on_page: Open the URL. The chapter opens with a "Summary → Clinical characteristics." subsection. Its second paragraph is one long sentence enumerating CF morbidities across organ systems. The clause cited below appears about three-quarters of the way through that sentence, between "diabetes" and "reduced fertility or infertility in some women".
evidence.exact_quote: Morbidities include ... male infertility due to hypoplasia or aplasia of the vas deferens, and reduced fertility or infertility in some women.
